title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an art restorer called Hollie Bennett talking about her work preserving famous paintings. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an art restorer called Hollie Bennett talking about her work preserving famous paintings. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Hollie Bennett discusses the ethical and technical aspects of cleaning and repairing valuable artworks.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello, I’m Hollie. My job is a strange mix of chemistry, history, and surgery. I’m a fine art restorer. My journey into this world didn't start in a museum; it actually began when I tried to [7]clean an old portrait[/7] I bought at a flea market when I was sixteen. I used the wrong solvent and nearly destroyed it. That mistake made me realize just how complex the science of paint really is. I decided there and then to learn how to do it properly.

The most controversial part of our work is cleaning. Over centuries, paintings accumulate layers of dust, smoke, and yellowed varnish. I’ve found that the [8]removal of varnish[/8] is the most nerve-wracking stage of any project. You have to find a chemical that will dissolve the old coating without touching the original paint layer underneath. It’s a slow, microscopic process that can take months of careful work. When you finally see the vibrant colors the artist intended, it’s a magical feeling.

We also have to deal with physical damage. Canvases can tear, and wood can warp. I’ve noticed that [9]humidity fluctuations[/9] are the primary cause of cracks in old masterpieces. If a museum’s climate control fails for even a few hours, the paint can start to flake off. We use specialized adhesives to 're-attach' these tiny flakes, often using a single-hair brush. It requires the steady hands of a surgeon.

Modern technology has transformed our field. Before we even touch a painting, we use X-rays to see what lies beneath the surface. I was amazed to find a [10]hidden landscape[/10] underneath a portrait I was working on last year. It turned out the artist had reused the canvas to save money. These discoveries provide incredible insights into the artist’s creative process and sometimes change the way we value the work.

Ethics are at the heart of everything we do. Our rule is 'reversibility'. I’ve found that using [11]synthetic resins[/11] for our repairs is much better than using traditional oils. This is because our work can be easily removed by future restorers if better techniques are developed. We never want our 'corrections' to become a permanent part of the history of the painting. We are just temporary caretakers.

Working in a conservation lab can be quite isolating. I’ve found that the [12]chemical fumes[/12] are the biggest occupational hazard we face. We have to work under large extraction hoods and wear protective masks for hours at a time. It’s not the glamorous, artistic life many people imagine, but the reward is seeing a piece of history preserved for another hundred years.

Looking back, the biggest change in the industry is the focus on 'preventative conservation'. I believe that [13]better lighting systems[/13] in galleries are more important than any restoration work. By reducing UV exposure, we can prevent the paint from fading in the first place. It’s much easier to protect a painting than it is to fix it.

Finally, I’m currently developing a [14]training module[/14] for museum curators on how to handle fragile works during transport. I want to make sure that these treasures are safe even when they are moving between cities. Thank you for your time.
